687798.content:
Hi everyone,

Recently, there was an update to the evaluation metric that fixed a bug that was causing binary answers to match as floats instead of exactly as strings. This update led to a drop of about 0.3-0.4 points on new submissions. (The update is in the verify() function of the linked notebook.)

So that the leaderboard will only reflect submissions using the new metric, I will be initiating a rescore on Monday. This rescore will only evaluate those submissions that both are still present on the Public Leaderboard and that were made on or before March 28th, that is, prior to the metric update. This will affect a little less than 2/3 of the current LB. Other submissions made on or before March 28th, I will invalidate. Submissions made after that time will be preserved.

Why are we not rescoring every submission? Unfortunately, this would amount to over 9000 rescores and would strain our available capacity of the special GPUs used in this competition. We believe the above approach is the best compromise, that both preserves the existing leaderboard and maintains GPU availability for the community going forward.

Please let us know if you have any questions or concerns below.

687798.content:
I want to confirm if the stored answer is 0234, is 234 the considered correct?

I remember someone wrote a comment asking this, but I think it has since been deleted.

import re
import math

def verify(stored_answer: str, predicted: str) -> bool:
    # Clean up strings
    stored_answer = stored_answer.strip()
    predicted = predicted.strip()

    # If the answer is a binary string, compare strictly as strings
    if re.fullmatch(r'[01]+', stored_answer):
        return predicted.lower() == stored_answer.lower()

    try:
        # Try to convert the answers to floating point numbers
        stored_num = float(stored_answer)
        predicted_num = float(predicted)
        # Use a small absolute tolerance for numbers near zero
        return math.isclose(stored_num, predicted_num, rel_tol=1e-2, abs_tol=1e-5)
    except Exception as e:
        print(e)
        # Fallback to case-insensitive string comparison
        return predicted.lower() == stored_answer.lower()

print(verify("0234", "234"))

Currently this is True.

replies.content:
One of the bit manipulation questions is:

In Alice's Wonderland, a secret bit manipulation rule transforms 8-bit binary numbers. The transformation involves operations like bit shifts, rotations, XOR, AND, OR, NOT, and possibly majority or choice functions.

Here are some examples of input -> output:
01100110 -> 00000001  
01111111 -> 10000001  
11111111 -> 10000011  
00011110 -> 00000000  
11000011 -> 10000011  
00100101 -> 10000000  
11100101 -> 10000011  
11100100 -> 00000011  
01011110 -> 00000001  

Now, determine the output for: 01011000

The given ground truth label is 1 only, but the expected output is 00000001. So I think the labels were created according to this format.

Also, some of the ground truth labels in the bit manipulation tasks are not 8-bit (e.g., 1, 111101, 1000100, 10111111, etc.). Even if we train the model to predict 8-bit outputs, we need the ground truth labels to be in the same format if we want to compute accurate metrics.

687798.content:
Bug Report: extract_final_answer regex fails when the ground truth answer contains a literal } character

The extract_final_answer function in the metric uses the following regex to extract answers from \boxed{}:

matches = re.findall(r'\boxed{([^}]*)(?:}|$)', text)

The pattern [^}]* stops at the first } character encountered. This works correctly for most problem types, but fails systematically for equation_symbolic problems where the answer itself can contain } as a literal symbol character.

Example: problem b50cf853

  • Ground truth answer: |?"} (four symbols: |, ?, ", })

  • The model is instructed to output \boxed{|?"}

  • In the raw output this appears as: \boxed{|?"}}

  • The regex [^}]* matches |?" and stops at the first }, extracting |?" instead of |?"}

  • Result: |?" ≠ |?"} → marked incorrect, even though the model produced the right answer

    Impact:

    94 out of 9500 problems in the training set (all equation_symbolic type) have answers containing }. These are impossible to answer correctly under the current extraction logic, regardless of model quality. This means the theoretical maximum accuracy is capped at approximately 99.0% instead of 100%.

    Suggested fix:

    Use a greedy/balanced-brace matching approach instead of [^}]*, for example:

    Match the LAST \boxed{…}, allowing nested braces

    matches = re.findall(r'\boxed{((?:[^{}]|{[^{}]}))}', text)

    Or alternatively, accept escaped braces like } inside boxed content.

Bug Report: extract_final_answer regex fails when the ground truth answer
  contains a literal } character

  The extract_final_answer function in the metric uses the following regex to
  extract answers from \boxed{}:

  matches = re.findall(r'\\boxed\{([^}]*)(?:\}|$)', text)

  The pattern [^}]* stops at the first } character encountered. This works
  correctly for most problem types, but fails systematically for
  equation_symbolic problems where the answer itself can contain } as a literal
  symbol character.

  Example: problem b50cf853

  - Ground truth answer: |?"} (four symbols: |, ?, ", })
  - The model is instructed to output \boxed{|?"}
  - In the raw output this appears as: \boxed{|?"}}
  - The regex [^}]* matches |?" and stops at the first }, extracting |?" instead
   of |?"}
  - Result: |?" ≠ |?"} → marked incorrect, even though the model produced the
  right answer

  Impact:

  94 out of 9500 problems in the training set (all equation_symbolic type) have
  answers containing }. These are impossible to answer correctly under the
  current extraction logic, regardless of model quality. This means the
  theoretical maximum accuracy is capped at approximately 99.0% instead of 100%.

  Suggested fix:

  Use a greedy/balanced-brace matching approach instead of [^}]*, for example:

  # Match the LAST \boxed{...}, allowing nested braces
  matches = re.findall(r'\\boxed\{((?:[^{}]|\{[^{}]*\})*)\}', text)

  Or alternatively, accept escaped braces like \} inside boxed content.

Information for First-Timers

New to NVIDIA Nemotron open model family and its NeMo open libraries? If you’re just starting to explore these models, tools, or docs, feel free to start a new thread in this channel, or drop any first-timer questions here and folks can help you get unblocked. If you’re new to Nemotron and NeMo, skim the official Nemotron model pages, NeMo docs, and the resources listed below, then come back with specific questions about setup, fine-tuning, RL, or evaluation. Happy reasoning with Nemotron, and good luck climbing the leaderboard!

1. Core Nemotron model family (models & baselines)

  • Nemotron overview (model family, capabilities, benchmarks)
  • Nemotron GitHub(main repo and examples)
  • Nemotron 3 Nano technical blog (architecture, efficiency, training details)
  • Nemotron v3 collection on Hugging Face (ready-to-use checkpoints)

2. Nemotron open libraries for data & RL

Synthetic data generation NeMo Data Designer (generate domain/task-specific synthetic data):

  • GitHub: https://github.com/NVIDIA-NeMo/DataDesigner
  • Docs: https://nvidia-nemo.github.io/DataDesigner/latest/
  • Recipes: https://nvidia-nemo.github.io/DataDesigner/latest/recipes/cards/

Data filtering and curation NeMo Curator (multi‑modal data curation: text, images, video, audio):

  • GitHub: https://github.com/NVIDIA-NeMo/Curator
  • Docs: https://docs.nvidia.com/nemo/curator/latest/

RL & Gym (reasoning, behavior, agents) NeMo RL (RL training at scale with SOTA algorithms):

  • GitHub: https://github.com/NVIDIA-NeMo/RL

NeMo Gym (build/manage RL environments)

  • GitHub: https://github.com/NVIDIA-NeMo/gym
  • Available environments: https://github.com/NVIDIA-NeMo/Gym?tab=readme-ov-file#-available-environments

Guides & tutorials Nemotron 3 Super – multi‑stage RLVR & RLHF recipe with NeMo RL:

  • Guide: https://github.com/NVIDIA-NeMo/RL/blob/super-v3/docs/guides/nemotron-3-super.md
  • GRPO RLVR Jupyter notebook: https://github.com/NVIDIA-NeMo/Nemotron/tree/main/usage-cookbook/Nemotron-3-Super/grpo-dapo

Nemotron 3 Nano RL guide https://docs.nvidia.com/nemo/rl/nightly/guides/nemotron-3-nano.html

3. Nemotron open datasets (instruction-following & reasoning)

  • Pre‑training datasets collection: Link
  • Post‑training datasets: Link
  • RL instruction‑following dataset: Link
  • Agentic dataset:Link
  • RAG dataset: Link
  • Personas collection: Link
  • More NVIDIA datasets: Link

4. Cookbooks, recipes, and evaluation

  • Nemotron main repo (cookbooks, training recipes, deployment examples): https://github.com/NVIDIA-NeMo/Nemotron
  • Nemotron 3 Nano reproducibility / eval guide with NeMo Evaluator: https://github.com/NVIDIA-NeMo/Evaluator/blob/main/packages/nemo-evaluator-launcher/examples/nemotron/nano-v3-reproducibility.md

5. Community & learning paths

If you get stuck, want feedback on an idea, or just want to see how others are approaching the challenge, these are great next stops:

Nemotron / NVIDIA Developer Discord (join + Nemotron channel): Join: https://discord.com/invite/nvidiadeveloper

Nemotron Reasoning Challenge Discord channel: https://discord.gg/udbkJyWk

NVIDIA community email (questions, feedback, sharing work): community@nvidia.com

Learning paths

  • Build an AI agent (guide + code): https://developer.nvidia.com/topics/ai/how-to-build-an-ai-agent
  • GitHub: https://github.com/NVIDIA-NeMo
  • Build a RAG agent (guide + code): https://developer.nvidia.com/topics/ai/how-to-build-agentic-ai-rag
  • GitHub: https://github.com/NVIDIA-NeMo
  • Launchable environment for Nemotron (pre‑configured cloud env): https://brev.nvidia.com/launchable/deploy?launchableID=env-32kC34ErT9wsqTcJyaKMxBEuhr2
  • GitHub: https://github.com/NVIDIA-NeMo
  • Nemotron Labs livestreams playlist (deep dives, demos, Q&A): https://youtube.com/playlist?list=PL5B692fm6--vEL0FwctKghCpyEnBGAQJA&si=jhVNZVfdj_91hpEJ

681745.content:
Hi Jamil,

There appears to be some trainining data bug which I identified lately, as the model was being trained on buggy data and gave wrong answer.

Below are the areas critical:

  • Bit manipulation: 50.5% of training traces are WRONG (764/1513 mismatches between computed Result and answer)
  • Equation transformation: 49% have I/O length mismatches making char_map fundamentally broken, 44% have unknown chars

Bit Manipulation:

1988 total training examples 1513 have a Result line 764 have MISMATCHES between computed Result and \boxed{} answer 50.5% mismatch rate - HALF the training data teaches WRONG reasoning! 344 examples have f() unknowns (where we couldn't determine the rule) Equation Transformation:

3157 total training examples

1555 (49%) have input/output lengths that differ - char_map approach is fundamentally wrong for these

Another 1382 (44%) have unknown '?' characters when applying the char_map to the query

77 (2%) the char_map fails to even predict any of the given examples

The pattern is unmistakable: bit_manipulation is poisoning the model with wrong answers in half its examples, and equation_transformation's char_map strategy simply doesn't work for nearly half the data due to length mismatches and missing characters.

Was this left on purpose ?

Is there any plan to fix these bugs or we don't intend to fix ?

I used this script to find the issues and bugs in training data.

I am also seeing some kind of 1% drop in scores.

If my understanding is correct, the update is to fix such cases, right? 

True answer: +}
Model output: \boxed{+}}
Old metric extracted: +
New metric extracts: +}
Result: now correct

But if for some reason the model added } at the end of boxed, like this : 

True answer: 42
Model output: \boxed{42}}
Old metric extracted: 42
New metric extracts: 42}
Result: wrong

Then our score will go down with the new update.

So maybe this is the case? The public LB does not have problems with "}" in the answer (or our models can't handle it correctly in the first place), but might have some outputs with multiple }} in the boxed output, which causes the wrong answer to other problems.

707856.content:
I have been testing a more conservative cryptarithm CoT direction that avoids making multiplication-pattern lookup the main learned behavior.

The short version:

  • Do not try to solve every cryptarithm problem.
  • Enter the structural reasoning path only when there are enough linearizable examples.
  • Use subtraction / signed-difference / addition-style examples as linear constraints.
  • Split each grouped equation into small carry branches.
  • Reject branches by visible integer/range/distinct contradictions.
  • Use multiplication examples only as verification when possible.
  • If the branch product or remaining candidate set becomes too large, roll back to a simple fallback instead of training a long unstable trace.

This is not a full solver. It is meant as a gated training target for the small subset where the reasoning trace can stay mechanical and explicit.

Motivation

The main failure mode I see with long cryptarithm CoT is hidden search.

For example, a trace like this is not very learnable:

84 candidates -> 1 candidate
one consistent map remains

It may be correct under a solver, but the model is being asked to imitate a hidden enumeration step. Teacher-forced loss can look acceptable while generation still fails.

The same issue appears with large multiplication pattern tables. A mul anchor is useful for a solver, but asking the model to reproduce a large candidate table plus many reject rows seems fragile.

So I tried to focus on cases where the puzzle exposes enough linear equations.

Linearizable examples

Suppose an example has the form:

AB - CD = -EF

After choosing a high-probability signed rule, this can be expanded as:

(10A + B) - (10C + D) = -(10E + F)

Move terms:

10A + B - 10C - D + 10E + F = 0

Group tens and ones:

10(A - C + E) = -B + D - F

The right side is a small digit expression. Since each digit is in 0..9, this expression often has only a few possible multiples of 10.

So instead of solving the whole mapping directly, split by carry:

10L = R

branch k:
L = k
R = 10k

For many signed/subtraction examples, k is usually one of:

-1, 0, 1

and often only two of these are actually possible from the range of R.

Four-equation gate

The important gate is the number of usable linear examples.

If we have only one or two equations, the constraints are usually too weak.

If we have four usable equations, then each equation contributes a small carry split. For example, if each equation has two possible carry values, the branch product is:

2^4 = 16 branches

That is small enough to show in a CoT.

Each branch gives two linear equations per example:

10L_i = R_i

becomes

L_i = k_i
R_i = 10k_i

With four grouped equations, this gives up to eight simple linear equations. Then we can eliminate variables or reject by contradictions.

Example trace shape

The training trace should expose the equations, not hide the solver.

For example:

Use default high-probability rules:
- signed output -> sub_signed
- unsigned length-3 output -> add

Read numbers in standard direction.
Let symbols map to digit variables:
!=a, $=b, &=c, '=d, (=e, /=f, >=g, [=h, ^=i, }=j

Linear example 1:
  left = 10a+b
  right = 10c+d
  out = -(10e+f)
  equation = left - right = out
  grouped equation = E1

E1:
  (10a+b) - (10c+d) = -(10e+f)
  10a + b - 10c - d + 10e + f = 0
  10(a-c+e) = -b+d-f
  so k1 in {-1,0}

Linear example 2:
  ...
  grouped equation = E2

Linear example 3:
  ...
  grouped equation = E3

Linear example 4:
  ...
  grouped equation = E4

Then enumerate branches:

Branch B1: k=(-1,-1,0,0)
  E1 split:
    a-c+e = -1
    -b+d-f = -10
  E2 split:
    ...
  E3 split:
    ...
  E4 split:
    ...

  Reject if:
    a digit is outside 0..9, or
    two symbols require the same digit, or
    a simple equation requires something impossible such as b-c=10.

A good rejection line should be explicit:

From E3:
  b - c = 10

But b and c are digits, so b-c is in [-9,9].
Therefore this branch is impossible.

That is more learnable than:

reject: no_digit_assignment

Candidate policy

The policy I think is safest:

if usable_linear_examples >= 4 and branch_count <= small_cap:
    expand linear equations
    enumerate carry branches
    reject impossible branches explicitly
    verify remaining candidates on all visible examples
    if unique verified answer:
        use structural answer
    else:
        rollback to fallback
else:
    fallback directly

The rollback is important. Problems with similar surface structure should not suddenly use completely different reasoning styles. They should enter the same gate, then fail the gate or fail verification and fall back.

Multiplication handling

I would not make multiplication pattern lookup the primary learned step.

For multiplication examples, I prefer using them after the linear branch has already reduced the digit map.

For example:

After the linear equations, candidate map C remains.
Verify multiplication example:
  left = ...
  right = ...
  output = ...
  left * right + k = output

If it verifies, keep the branch. If it fails, reject the branch.

This avoids asking the model to generate a large multiplication-pattern candidate table.

Why this may be safer than long reject traces

The model only has to learn a small number of repeated mechanical moves:

  1. Parse two-digit numbers into digit variables.
  2. Expand linear examples.
  3. Group into 10L = R.
  4. Split by carry k.
  5. Reject by range / integer / distinct-digit contradictions.
  6. Verify visible examples.
  7. Fall back when the branch space is too large.

The subset solved this way is small, but the trace is more learnable. It is closer to a repeated algebra template than to a hidden brute-force solver.

My current view is that this should be used as a gated partial strategy, not as a replacement for the original cryptarithm fallback. The main risk is making the trace too long or hiding too much elimination. If the CoT contains compressed statements like one consistent map remains, it probably becomes solver replay rather than learnable reasoning.

Curious if anyone else has tried this kind of carry-split linear gate for cryptarithm, especially with a strict rollback when the branch count is too large.

708212.content:
TL;DR: On the public leaderboard, 1528 teams are tied at exactly 0.86 — and the bronze cutline falls inside that single bar. So matching the public 0.86 notebook is necessary but not sufficient for a medal. Here's the full distribution and what it means.

(histogram attached below)

The distribution (n = 4233 teams): 0.90×1, 0.88×3, 0.87×43, 0.86×1528, 0.85×520, 0.84×245, then a long tail.

The medal math. Cutline ranks for a 1000+ team competition are bronze top 10%, silver top 5%, gold top 10 + 0.2%. For 4233 teams that is gold ~rank 18 (=0.87), silver ~211 (=0.86), bronze ~423 (=0.86). The 0.86 cluster spans ranks 48–1575, so the bronze cut lands inside it. Being at 0.86 doesn't by itself secure a medal — your spot within that ~1500-team tie (earliest submission date) plus the private-LB shake decides it. If you reach 0.86 today, you're near the back of the tie.

The frontier is private. Above the commodity, 0.87 has only 47 teams and 0.88 only 3. The public notebooks top out at the 0.86 recipe. A public 0.88 training script I examined actually depends on a private distilled dataset plus a private warm-start adapter chain — so forking it won't reproduce 0.88.

What broke my own ceiling (a reusable lever). My straight forks of the public 0.86 recipe were high-variance (0.81–0.84 on identical config) and never reproduced 0.86. What finally moved my long-stuck 0.84 → 0.85 was two cheap, general levers:

  1. Self-authored deterministic-solver synthetic CoT for the rule-based categories (bit-manipulation, cipher, numeral, equation), generated in-kernel, each ending in \boxed{answer} and verified correct by construction. Mixed as a minority by tokens, so the verbose real CoT still dominates (terse synthetic, over-represented, can collapse the model).
  2. 5× loss upweighting on the final \boxed{} answer tokens — the exact tokens the metric scores.

Scaling the synthetic further (2.5× data, 2 epochs) saturated at 0.85 — the data-quality path plateaus.

Honest limitation: I did not medal. 0.85 is below the cut, and I couldn't reach 0.86 reliably in my setup. Sharing this so others don't burn GPU re-discovering that (a) matching the public 0.86 late won't medal, and (b) the true frontier is private.

Is the public/private gap this stark in other code competitions too? Curious how others read their own LB distributions.

708244.content:
Spent most of last week trying to figure out if cryptarithm is really the moat everyone keeps pointing at, instead of just arguing about it. Posting what I found, mostly so nobody else burns a day on it like I did.

I wrote two solvers that try to derive the symbol→digit mapping and the operators straight from the examples. One quick and dumb, one that handles the weirder operator layouts and gives up when a puzzle has more than one valid solution. Ran both on the 399 val puzzles:

  • the dumb one solved 0. literally zero. only 2 puzzles even had the operator in a consistent spot.
  • the better one got 18/399, about 4.5%, and that's with a pile of timeouts.

So most of these just aren't determined. The examples don't pin the mapping down.

Here's the part that actually made me drop it though: the base model already scores ~7-8% on crypt just by guessing / assuming concatenation. My solver only manages 4.5%. So if you train on properly-derived crypt CoT, you might be teaching it to do worse than it already does on its own.

I think that's also what's behind the "I got crypt from 8% to 70% but my LB didn't budge" posts. The only way I could get high local accuracy was writing traces that just state the mapping (which my generator obviously already knew). That memorizes your local set and transfers nothing.

I'm not claiming this is impossible, just that my solvers with my assumptions couldn't do better, and it's only ~8% of the score by count anyway. Maybe someone solves the determinate ones and guesses the rest. If you've got a crypt approach that actually transfers I'd genuinely love to hear it, because I struck out.

Quick illustration of the failure mode. Say the rule is "⊕ = concatenate the operands": ◆ ⊕ ▲ = ◆▲ ▲ ⊕ ◆ = ▲◆ ◆ ⊕ ● = ?

You can figure out ⊕ is concat, but ● never shows up in the examples, so its digit is anyone's guess. And this isn't hypothetical and several of the actual puzzles have a query symbol that appears in none of the examples.

Yes, this industry chain is actually quite mature in China. A lot of Chinese undergrads apply for one-year master's programs abroad right after graduation. Most of them don't have any impressive competition results or academic research under their belts, so paying for a competition award endorsed by a foreign professor becomes a pretty attractive option. 😂

I actually teamed up with someone else before. It was about a year ago, right after I played my first Kaggle competition and got a bronze medal. He was a really good friend of mine, and I wanted to help him out since he was also planning to apply for grad school overseas. Unfortunately, we didn't win anything that time—we missed the bronze zone by just a few spots.🙃

Honestly, experiences like that make people who want to prove their technical skills through Kaggle pretty hesitant to team up with others. We'd rather just go solo.🧐

708253.content:
Hi everyone,

I wanted to share a small contribution I’ve been working on for the Nemotron Reasoning Challenge - while its use remains at 0.63 public score within short epoches runs (2-3 Epochs). However, I developed Pro-SAT (Programmatic Solver-Augmented Traces), a fully rule-based system that generates Chain-of-Thought reasoning traces without using any LLM calls. The approach reverse-engineers the underlying transformation rules from the puzzle examples and applies them deterministically to create verifiable reasoning traces. The current version supports the six puzzle types in the Alice in Wonderland dataset and produces traces formatted for Nemotron models (ending in \boxed{}).

Datasets

I’ve made the generated traces available in two places:

  • Kaggle Dataset: Nemotron Atlas Solver Augmented Traces Script
  • Hugging Face Dataset: Pro-SAT - Alice in Wonderland Traces

Pros of this approach

  • Solvers Coverage (please double check in case I misinterpreted any) : --- Cipher: 100%, Numeral 100%, Gravity 100%, Unit Conversion 100% --- Binary puzzles ~ 97% --- Equation Transform ~ 44%

  • Fully deterministic and reproducible

  • Reduce risk of hallucination in the reasoning traces

  • Traces are independently verifiable

  • Efficient token usage (fits well within 768–1024 tokens)

  • Can be scaled programmatically without API costs

Cons & Limitations

  • Currently limited to the six supported puzzle types
  • Rule-based systems can be brittle when encountering edge cases
  • Less flexible compared to LLM-generated reasoning data
  • May not capture more creative or multi-step reasoning patterns that LLMs sometimes produce
  • Weak Traces for equation_transform

I’m sharing this in the hope that it can be useful to others, and I would greatly appreciate any feedback, ideas, or collaboration from the community.

If anyone has thoughts on how to improve the solver logic, extend it to more puzzle types, or combine it with LLM-generated data, I’d love to hear your insights.

Thank you!

Best regards,
Shehab Anwer (HabAnwer)

707987.content:
I'm trying to understand how people are practically training Nemotron on the RTX Pro 6000 when working with long CoT datasets.

After auditing my dataset, I found:

  • 8708 verified traces
  • ~8.2M total tokens
  • Full expert LoRA enabled (~888M trainable parameters)
  • Bit manipulation average length ≈ 6958 tokens
  • Cipher average length ≈ 2862 tokens

When I checked truncation at MAX_SEQ_LENGTH=2048, I discovered:

  • bit_manipulation: 100% truncated
  • cipher: 79% truncated

So training at 2048 seems to throw away a significant portion of the reasoning traces.

My confusion is that several high-ranking solutions appear to be training on RTX Pro 6000 GPUs, yet the competition allows up to 8192 context and many traces naturally approach that length.

How are people handling this in practice?

  • Training at 4096–8192 with microbatch=1?
  • Sequence packing?
  • Dataset filtering/compression?
  • Custom loops (cut cross entropy, etc.)?
  • Fewer epochs with longer context?

I can fit the model(at 2048 ) , but once I enabled expert LoRA (~888M params), training time became a major bottleneck and I'm trying to understand which optimization path is actually being used by successful teams.

Any insights would be hugely appreciated.

* Bit manipulation average length ≈ 6958 tokens
* Cipher average length ≈ 2862 tokens

When I checked truncation at MAX_SEQ_LENGTH=2048, I discovered:

* bit_manipulation: 100% truncated
* cipher: 79% truncated

So training at 2048 seems to throw away a significant portion of the reasoning traces.

My confusion is that several high-ranking solutions appear to be training on RTX Pro 6000 GPUs, yet the competition allows up to 8192 context and many traces naturally approach that length.

How are people handling this in practice?

* Training at 4096–8192 with microbatch=1?
* Sequence packing?
* Dataset filtering/compression?
* Custom loops (cut cross entropy, etc.)?
* Fewer epochs with longer context?

I can fit the model(at 2048 ) , but once I enabled expert LoRA (~888M params), training time became a major bottleneck and I'm trying to understand which optimization path is actually being used by successful teams.

Any insights would be hugely appreciated.

replies.content:
The official competition data (train.csv) has no category column — categories are never disclosed. What you're seeing as "6 categories" are actually 6 scoring buckets inferred from problem content. Huikang's analysis revealed 16 fine-grained types that map into those buckets:

Bucket Fine-grained categories
bit_like bit_manipulation
cipher_like cipher, spelling
numeral_like numeral
unit_like unit_conversion
equation_like equation_numeric, cryptarithm_guess, cryptarithm_deduce, equation_numeric_deduce, equation_numeric_guess, equation_symbolic
other matching, splitting, concatenation, gravity, lstrip

The 7 you listed are the "obvious" ones visible from the problem prompts — but the test set includes hidden categories that don't appear in standard training data: matching, splitting, concatenation, spelling, lstrip, equation_symbolic. Training without those means scoring 0 on a large fraction of the test problems regardless of how well you tune hyperparameters.

This is what huikang's work actually revealed — not just more training examples, but the existence of test-only problem types the competition didn't tell you about.

707802.content:
Update — Root Cause Confirmed, Plus Two Additional Findings

Short answer: the dead path is confirmed, the exact guard is identified, and there is a related packaging issue that may affect anyone submitting expert LoRA.

1. Root cause confirmed: UnslothCheckpointFunction backward guard

After inspecting the step-150 and step-300 checkpoints, all 23 mixer.out_proj.lora_B.weight tensors were exactly zero — not small, not slowly converging, exactly zero. lora_A was static at its Kaiming-uniform initialization value (max=0.01562), identical across all 23 layers. This indicates zero gradient, not merely slow learning.

The root cause is in UnslothCheckpointFunction.forward():

# Unsloth gradient-checkpoint forward (paraphrased)
if args.requires_grad:
    # enable autograd context → backward works
else:
    return None  # backward returns None → zero gradient for all params in this block

The Mamba SSM fused CUDA kernel computes the SSM scan and produces an output tensor that does not carry requires_grad=True after passing through the kernel. That scan output becomes args[0], which is the input to out_proj. Because it lacks requires_grad, the guard fires, backward is suppressed, and out_proj.lora_A / out_proj.lora_B receive zero gradient for the entire run.

By contrast, in_proj receives the layer's hidden states as input, and those tensors consistently have requires_grad=True flowing from earlier layers. As a result, gradients flow normally there. This matches the observed behavior: in_proj.lora_B trained successfully (max 0.010–0.054 at step 150), while out_proj.lora_B remained zero.

This appears to be the same mechanism reported in unsloth/unsloth#5039 for vision LoRA on Gemma 4, where requires_grad_for_gradient_checkpointing() hooks the wrong parameter and causes the backward guard to fire on scan outputs that lack requires_grad.

Fix applied: out_proj has been removed from target_modules for future runs. The 46 dead keys (23 lora_A + 23 lora_B, all zero) are now stripped at packaging time, since they increase file size without providing any inference benefit.

2. Second finding: prepare_model_for_training silently freezes custom-named LoRA params

Separately from the out_proj dead path, another issue showed up in Unsloth's prepare_model_for_training (unsloth_zoo/training_utils.py). It freezes any parameter whose name does not contain .lora_A. or .lora_B. with dots on both sides:

if ".lora_A." in name or ".lora_B." in name or ".lora_magnitude_vector" in name:
    requires_grad = True
else:
    requires_grad = False

Per-expert LoRA was injected as nn.Parameter objects named lora_A_up, lora_B_up, lora_A_down, and lora_B_down directly onto NemotronHExperts. Because the underscore-suffixed names (lora_A_up, not lora_A.default) do not match the dot-delimited pattern, all 92 expert LoRA tensors were silently frozen for several runs (27.7M trainable instead of 883M), with no error or warning.

Fix: explicitly call requires_grad_(True) on those four parameter basenames after get_peft_model() returns.

3. AhHa: Kaggle's NemotronH evaluator uses unfused experts

This is likely the most important finding for anyone working with MoE expert LoRA in this competition.

A starter notebook showed that standard PEFT with:

target_modules = r".*\.(up_proj|down_proj)$"

yields 880M trainable parameters on the Kaggle-side model. That is only possible if Kaggle's NemotronH evaluation environment exposes 128 individual nn.Linear modules per MoE layer (for example, mixer.experts.{j}.up_proj) that are visible to PEFT's named-module traversal.

The training environment (Unsloth on GB10) uses the fused path instead: NemotronHExperts stores all 128 expert weights as a single [128, out, in] tensor, with no individual nn.Linear per expert. Per-expert LoRA was therefore injected via custom nn.Parameter tensors, and forward() was patched to apply them.

The consequence is that expert_lora_weights.pt was stored as fused [128, r, dim] tensors and never converted to PEFT format before submission. That means 856M trained expert LoRA parameters were unused on every Kaggle inference call.

Fix: package_submission.sh now splits each fused tensor into 128 per-expert PEFT keys before zipping:

Input:  layers.{i}.mixer.experts.lora_A_up        
Output: layers.{i}.mixer.experts.{j}.up_proj.lora_A.weight    (× 128 experts)

That injects 23 layers × 128 experts × 4 tensors = 11,776 new keys into adapter_model.safetensors.

Run13 (currently training, around step 20 of 1000) is the first run intended to close this gap end-to-end.

TL;DR

  1. out_proj LoRA is silently dead — remove it from target_modules.
  2. Custom-named nn.Parameter LoRA (anything not matching .lora_A. with dots) is silently frozen by prepare_model_for_training — manually re-enable requires_grad after get_peft_model().
  3. If training uses the fused expert path (GB10 / Unsloth), expert LoRA must be converted from [E, r, dim] fused tensors into per-expert PEFT keys before submission.
707802.rawMarkdown:
## Update — Root Cause Confirmed, Plus Two Additional Findings

**Short answer:** the dead path is confirmed, the exact guard is identified, and there is a related packaging issue that may affect anyone submitting expert LoRA.

### 1. Root cause confirmed: `UnslothCheckpointFunction` backward guard

After inspecting the step-150 and step-300 checkpoints, all 23 `mixer.out_proj.lora_B.weight` tensors were exactly zero — not small, not slowly converging, exactly zero. `lora_A` was static at its Kaiming-uniform initialization value (`max=0.01562`), identical across all 23 layers. This indicates zero gradient, not merely slow learning.

The root cause is in `UnslothCheckpointFunction.forward()`:

```python
# Unsloth gradient-checkpoint forward (paraphrased)
if args.requires_grad:
    # enable autograd context → backward works
else:
    return None  # backward returns None → zero gradient for all params in this block
```

The Mamba SSM fused CUDA kernel computes the SSM scan and produces an output tensor that does not carry `requires_grad=True` after passing through the kernel. That scan output becomes `args[0]`, which is the input to `out_proj`. Because it lacks `requires_grad`, the guard fires, backward is suppressed, and `out_proj.lora_A` / `out_proj.lora_B` receive zero gradient for the entire run.

By contrast, `in_proj` receives the layer's hidden states as input, and those tensors consistently have `requires_grad=True` flowing from earlier layers. As a result, gradients flow normally there. This matches the observed behavior: `in_proj.lora_B` trained successfully (`max 0.010–0.054` at step 150), while `out_proj.lora_B` remained zero.

This appears to be the same mechanism reported in `unsloth/unsloth#5039` for vision LoRA on Gemma 4, where `requires_grad_for_gradient_checkpointing()` hooks the wrong parameter and causes the backward guard to fire on scan outputs that lack `requires_grad`.

**Fix applied:** `out_proj` has been removed from `target_modules` for future runs. The 46 dead keys (23 `lora_A` + 23 `lora_B`, all zero) are now stripped at packaging time, since they increase file size without providing any inference benefit.

### 2. Second finding: `prepare_model_for_training` silently freezes custom-named LoRA params

Separately from the `out_proj` dead path, another issue showed up in `Unsloth`'s `prepare_model_for_training` (`unsloth_zoo/training_utils.py`). It freezes any parameter whose name does not contain `.lora_A.` or `.lora_B.` with dots on both sides:

```python
if ".lora_A." in name or ".lora_B." in name or ".lora_magnitude_vector" in name:
    requires_grad = True
else:
    requires_grad = False
```

Per-expert LoRA was injected as `nn.Parameter` objects named `lora_A_up`, `lora_B_up`, `lora_A_down`, and `lora_B_down` directly onto `NemotronHExperts`. Because the underscore-suffixed names (`lora_A_up`, not `lora_A.default`) do not match the dot-delimited pattern, all 92 expert LoRA tensors were silently frozen for several runs (`27.7M` trainable instead of `883M`), with no error or warning.

**Fix:** explicitly call `requires_grad_(True)` on those four parameter basenames after `get_peft_model()` returns.

### 3. AhHa: Kaggle's `NemotronH` evaluator uses unfused experts

This is likely the most important finding for anyone working with MoE expert LoRA in this competition.

A starter notebook showed that standard PEFT with:

```python
target_modules = r".*\.(up_proj|down_proj)$"
```

yields `880M` trainable parameters on the Kaggle-side model. That is only possible if Kaggle's `NemotronH` evaluation environment exposes 128 individual `nn.Linear` modules per MoE layer (for example, `mixer.experts.{j}.up_proj`) that are visible to PEFT's named-module traversal.

The training environment (`Unsloth` on GB10) uses the fused path instead: `NemotronHExperts` stores all 128 expert weights as a single `[128, out, in]` tensor, with no individual `nn.Linear` per expert. Per-expert LoRA was therefore injected via custom `nn.Parameter` tensors, and `forward()` was patched to apply them.

The consequence is that `expert_lora_weights.pt` was stored as fused `[128, r, dim]` tensors and never converted to PEFT format before submission. That means `856M` trained expert LoRA parameters were unused on every Kaggle inference call.

**Fix:** `package_submission.sh` now splits each fused tensor into 128 per-expert PEFT keys before zipping:

```text
Input:  layers.{i}.mixer.experts.lora_A_up        
Output: layers.{i}.mixer.experts.{j}.up_proj.lora_A.weight    (× 128 experts)
```

That injects `23 layers × 128 experts × 4 tensors = 11,776` new keys into `adapter_model.safetensors`.

`Run13` (currently training, around step 20 of 1000) is the first run intended to close this gap end-to-end.

### TL;DR

1. `out_proj` LoRA is silently dead — remove it from `target_modules`.
2. Custom-named `nn.Parameter` LoRA (anything not matching `.lora_A.` with dots) is silently frozen by `prepare_model_for_training` — manually re-enable `requires_grad` after `get_peft_model()`.
3. If training uses the fused expert path (GB10 / Unsloth), expert LoRA must be converted from `[E, r, dim]` fused tensors into per-expert PEFT keys before submission.

707802.content:
While inspecting checkpoint weights mid-training on the NemotronH 30B model with Unsloth, I ran into a consistent LoRA issue I have not seen documented:

  • All mixer.out_proj.lora_B.weight tensors are exactly zero at step 150 across all 23 Mamba SSM layers.
  • The corresponding lora_A weights are stuck at their Kaiming initialization (e.g., max=0.01562 identical across all layers), indicating zero gradient flow rather than slow learning.
  • In contrast, all mixer.in_proj.lora_B weights train normally (max roughly 0.010–0.054 by step 150), and attention q/k/v/o_proj plus MoE shared-expert LoRA weights also update as expected.

Setup

  • Model: nvidia/NVIDIA-Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B-BF16
  • Framework: Unsloth + PEFT SFT training on DGX Spark (GB10, 128 GB unified memory)
  • LoRA config: target_modules explicitly includes out_proj (keys confirmed in the adapter safetensors)
  • NemotronH patch: _patch_mamba_fastpath(model) applied (required for NemotronH on GB10)

Hypothesis

Unsloth’s Mamba fast-path appears to use a fused CUDA kernel that calls the underlying out_proj weight tensor directly, bypassing the PEFT LoraLinear forward wrapper. As a result, out_proj(x) never flows through the Python module that houses lora_A/lora_B, so autograd never constructs gradient edges for those parameters, leaving them at initialization for the entire run. The in_proj LoRA works because the SSM scan still consumes its output at Python level before the fused kernel executes.

Current workaround

I’ve removed out_proj from target_modules. This avoids allocating ~3M “dead” LoRA parameters and reduces adapter size, with no observable change in training dynamics so far.

Questions

  1. Has anyone else reproduced this with NemotronH + Unsloth, especially with _patch_mamba_fastpath enabled? Does the issue disappear if you disable or bypass the fast-path?
  2. Is there a supported way to force out_proj to go through the PEFT wrapper while keeping the Mamba fast-path enabled for the rest of the layer?
  3. Are there other Mamba SSM submodules that Unsloth’s fused kernels might be bypassing in a similar way that I should double-check for dead LoRA weights?

705393.content:
I’ve been working through the NemotronH LoRA adapter submission process and hit an interesting constraint I’m hoping others have thoughts on.

Background

NemotronH has two types of MoE expert layers per block:

  • Shared experts (mixer.shared_experts.up_proj / down_proj) — standard nn.Linear; every token passes through these
  • Routed (main) experts (mixer.experts.*) — 1-of-N dispatch per token; Unsloth fuses all expert weight matrices into a single tensor internally

When training with Unsloth’s FastLanguageModel, the routed experts get LoRA applied via a fused representation, saving keys like
mixer.experts.lora_A.weight (shape: [n_experts × rank, hidden]).

Standard HuggingFace PEFT has no equivalent module to attach these to, so submitting them causes an error from the evaluator.

What I’m currently submitting

140 keys: 48 attention (q/k/v/o_proj) + 92 shared expert (up/down_proj). These load cleanly with standard PEFT.

Confirmed working: run scores of 0.54, 0.54, 0.56 across three incremental SFT runs.

The question

Are the 46 routed-expert keys actually usable by the competition evaluator? A few possibilities I’ve considered:

  1. The evaluator is purely standard PEFT — routed expert keys are unsubmittable, and we just live with 140 keys
  2. Someone has found a way to convert Unsloth’s fused expert keys back to per-expert LoRA matrices that standard PEFT can load
  3. The evaluator actually uses Unsloth under the hood, and the fused keys would work (which might explain why some high-scoring adapters are larger)

Has anyone successfully submitted adapters with MoE expert LoRA included? If so, what key format did you use?

Curious what others are seeing — it feels like there’s potentially meaningful model capacity being left on the table if the routed experts can’t be included.
